title: Asynchronous Architecture Decisions for Go Services · Language Lens: Choreography Orchestration
summary: A Go 1.26 service team must make coupling, buffering, fan-out, replay, and failure costs observable at Go service boundaries; reviewers reject hidden goroutine ownership, unbounded work, stale channel state, and claims that stop at a nil client error. The learner predicts values, ownership transitions, and stop paths before seeing a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the choreography orchestration increment in a Go service coupling and broker-fit decision record. Learners progress through…
objectives: Choose choreography or orchestration by workflow visibility, policy ownership, compensation, evolution, and failure diagnosis needs; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.

Evaluate request/reply over messaging against ordinary HTTP using correlation, timeout, cancellation, failure ambiguity, and capacity evidence; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and Go Predict Choreography Orchestration · Theory Choreography Orchestration
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go messaging team handles case 6535 during canary Go consumer decoding old and new envelopes. Build deterministic Go evidence to choose HTTP, work distribution, pub/sub, or retained streams from coupling evidence; reject stale connection and channel generations; then reuse a message identity with a different payload fingerprint. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. amqp091-go, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, race and leak checks, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.
